title: Add add-signature and verify-signature
body: Add add-signature and verify-signature commands, for adding a signature to an image in its current location, and for verifying an image in place.

comments.body:
The use case, at the time, was checking that the storage backend implementation was fully functional in terms of signing and verifying images, preferably without implicitly depending on the feature being supported in other backends.

I could see add-signature being useful in a future registry setup, but yeah, I was hedging on the wording there.  I could also see verify-signature being used to defer the signature check until, or repeat the signature check when, we actually try to start up a container using the image, if we want to allow for things like key revocation, but that wasn't on my mind at the time.

Okay, other than whether we produce error messages using the passed-in image reference or a value retrived from `transports.ImageName()`, I think all feedback has been incorporated.  If I've missed something, though, please remind me.

> The use case, at the time, was checking that the storage backend implementation was fully functional in terms of signing and verifying images, preferably without implicitly depending on the feature being supported in other backends.

That’s a fairly weak reason to expose this (or, to be precise, _exactly_ this) to users… using the `dir:` backend as a counterpart, or writing one-off throwaway versions of the tools, or testing in unit tests, could work for those purposes without adding a CLI.

(The case for `standalone-sign` and `standalone-verify` is similarly weak, better only because these building blocks are the bare minimum and once they are present, everything else can be built in shell scripts.)

> I could see add-signature being useful in a future registry setup, but yeah, I was hedging on the wording there.

Yeah, I’m sure there are workflows where it will be extremely useful, but I am worried about someone just calling `skopeo add-signature docker://mycompany/importantproduct $supertrustedkey` without realizing that they aren’t in control of what they are signing. How do we nudge users to protect themselves?

Some possibilities:
- Do a signature check on the original image before signing it. (That can get naively self-referential - `add-signature` would refuse to sign by $key because it wants a signature by $key first.)
- Require the user to explicitly specify the image digest, with a very explicit name like `--manifest-digest-I-know-is-expected` (I suck at naming).

Perhaps both? Then the user can either sign images accepted by the policy (`skopeo add-signature --reference example.com/production-repo/product:$version atomic://qa-repo/product:$version $production-key`), or sign images with an explicit reference (`skopeo add-signature --manifest-digest-I-know-is-expected $digest atomic://production-repo/product:$version $production_key`).

> I could also see verify-signature being used to defer the signature check until, or repeat the signature check when, we actually try to start up a container using the image, if we want to allow for things like key revocation, but that wasn't on my mind at the time.

I’d kind of expect these checks to be done by Go code instead of calling an executable, but exposing re-checking to shell scripts could work. But I do strongly think that such re-verification should happen against the policy, not against a single specified digest+reference. (That’s actually simpler to implement, see `IsRunningImageAllowed` in `containers/image/copy/copy.go`.)

And again, how do we nudge users not to do `skopeo verify docker://mycompany/importantproduct` and then assume that further references to that are OK? One possibility would be to refuse to verify remote repositories but to still allow local ones (`dir:`, `docker-daemon:` etc.)—the local ones are still racy but presumably under the same administrative control; though that feels pretty draconian, there should be a better way.
Is this pull request still something we are considering?

Something a bit like `add-signature` was suggested as useful for OpenShift, but OpenShift would more likely want a Go API than a CLI; and in the mean time the `ImageDestination` API has been interpreted more restrictively in that just calling a `PutSignatures` without anything else is not necessarily legal; so the bulk of the work would be in changing this API rather than the CLI.

As for `verify-signature`, I still have concerns about TOCTOU vulnerabilities; and the suggested use case of verifying signatures on container start etc. will be handled differently (again by calling a Go API—and one specialized for verifying signatures of already-extracted images, at that).
@nalind @mtrmac This needs to be updated or closed
I am closing since no one has touched in another 6 months.
